coredata.dc:title: Thermal study on a LTO cell module: Experiment, 3D numerical analysis and model order reduction
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zatta, Nicolò
Bonanno, Giovanni
Trovò, Andrea
Cristofoli, Giovanni
Mattavelli, Paolo
Guarnieri, Massimo
coredata.dc:description: Understanding and controlling the temperature evolution of Lithium-ion batteries is crucial to maintain high performance, ensuring long cycling life and avoiding thermal abuse. This paper presents a numerical and experimental thermal analysis of an air-cooled industrial module provided with 20 prismatic lithium-titanate-oxide cells. First, a 3D numerical model is presented for studying the dynamic distribution of the module temperature when the cooling fans are turned on or off. The numerical results are validated against test bench measurements. The flow field investigation explains the uneven temperature distribution among cells. The computation in natural convection inside the module, i.e. with fans off, was resolved by means of a fine empirical tuning. Building on the results of the 3D model, a 0D lumped model has been developed resorting to a model order reduction (MOR) technique and an energy balance differential equation. The model was characterized by tuning the module experimental data coming from a straight-forward testing protocol. The 0D MOR model, implemented on Simulink, demonstrated capable of quickly predicting the highest cell temperatures, allowing an easy and precise control of the module temperature, with an error < 1°C.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TMS
Thermal management system
Lithium-ion battery
LTO
Prismatic cell
Cell module
Battery air cooling
Thermal lumped model
Thermal simulation
CFD

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Module 3-D air flow and thermal model
Experimental calibration of the MOR parameters

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A picture (a) and a scheme with dimensions (in mm) (b) of the 20-A h LTO prismatic cell under investigation; the 48V battery stack with bonnet, walls and cables (c) and the position of thermocouples array in the module (d).

Representation of the cells stack (without the bonnet, rods, fans and grilles) with the vertical symmetry plane used for 3D modeling from the front (a) and from the bottom (b): the upper and lower polymeric case (blue), the cell terminals and connectors (green), the fans housings (pink) with the air inlets (1), the external aluminium wall (orange) and the cells (gray). Air outlet are positioned on the grilles (2). In (c) (front) and (d) (bottom) the 20 thermocouples position is reported (red): sA and sB are considered for model validation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Test bench: (1) Cell module with dark bonnet, (2) Dana Power Supplies D2AX-M 85-75 cycler, (3) fans controller, (4) interface opto-isolators of the cell signals, (5) thermocouple signals preprocessing unit.
The cell voltage measured during the discharge GITT test at ambient temperature. An average resistance equal to 1.5mΩ was assumed to model the internal cell heat general term.
Mesh independence study for the fans on case: the variation of sB probe temperature at t equal to 1620s varying the mesh elements number is lower than 1%.

Validation tests for the fans on (a) and fans off (b) modes. Temperature evolution during a 80 min constant-current discharge–charge cycle. The experimental and simulated sA and sB probes temperatures are plotted.

Computed fields on the middle horizontal half plane in the fans on at t = 1560s during the 40A test (fans inlet on the right, grilles outlet on the left): (a) velocity magnitude map, (b) temperature map.

Air velocity streamlines and internal surfaces temperature color map at t = 1530s of the 40A discharge validation profile in fans on mode. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Results for resting phases from the experimental tests Ide-Test1 (a), Ide-Test2 (b), Ide-Test3 (c), Ide-Test4 (d) and interpolated exponential curves with their upper and lower boundaries.
Comparison between experimental validation tests and the dynamic behavior obtained from the proposed model for Val–Test1 (a), Val–Test2 (b), Val–Test3 (c), and Val–Test4 (d).

Plots in (a) and (b) relate to Val Test 2. These respectively report the comparison between the experimental results and the analytical model where
T

is replaced by

).

.

head.ce:title: Thermal study on a LTO cell module: Experiment, 3D numerical analysis and model order reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Engineering, University of Padua, Padua, 35131, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Industrial Engineering, University of Padua

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=Department of Industrial Engineering, University of Padua, city=Padua, postcode=35131, country=Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Management and Engineering, University of Padua

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=Department of Management and Engineering, University of Padua, city=Vicenza, postcode=36100, country=Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: FIAMM Energy Technology S.p.A., Montecchio Maggiore, 36075, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=FIAMM Energy Technology S.p.A., city=Montecchio Maggiore, postcode=36075, country=Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Understanding and controlling the temperature evolution of Lithium-ion batteries is crucial to maintain high performance, ensuring long cycling life and avoiding thermal abuse. This paper presents a numerical and experimental thermal analysis of an air-cooled industrial module provided with 20 prismatic lithium-titanate-oxide cells. First, a 3D numerical model is presented for studying the dynamic distribution of the module temperature when the cooling fans are turned on or off. The numerical results are validated against test bench measurements. The flow field investigation explains the uneven temperature distribution among cells. The computation in natural convection inside the module, i.e. with fans off, was resolved by means of a fine empirical tuning. Building on the results of the 3D model, a 0D lumped model has been developed resorting to a model order reduction (MOR) technique and an energy balance differential equation. The model was characterized by tuning the module experimental data coming from a straight-forward testing protocol. The 0D MOR model, implemented on Simulink, demonstrated capable of quickly predicting the highest cell temperatures, allowing an easy and precise control of the module temperature, with an error < 1°C.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
The transportation sector is becoming more and more electricified and Li-ion batteries are its dominant energy source [1], thanks to their high energy and power densities and good cycle life. Heat generation from internal losses is unavoidable in electrochemical cells, including Li-ion, so managing effectively the heat transfer in cell modules (also named stacks) is crucial to maintain the cells in an optimal temperature range, between 15°C and 60°C, not only to prevent thermal instability and runaway which can result in battery and vehicle fire but also to ensure higher battery performance and to avoid unbalanced cell operation which can impair the battery state of health and cycle life [2]. The battery management system (BMS), and more in particular the battery thermal management system (TMS), is the electronic circuit that provides such control, maintaining the cell currents and temperatures far from critical values and as balanced as possible [3], typically with differences Δ
T
< 6°C [4]. Accurate 3D thermal modeling of a cell module can yield helpful knowledge in designing an effective cooling system chosen among the different known types (e.g., air cooling, liquid cooling, phase-change materials [5]) and also in tuning performative thermal control approaches. Various strategies have been proposed in the literature to model the cell thermal generation and heat transfer inside the module [6]. Batteries can be modeled as simple pure-ohmic resistors, as in [7]. This results in easy to characterize models that could fit the need for large thermal simulations: on the contrary, accuracy is often low and little information could be obtained on voltage prediction. More accurate are the equivalent electrical circuit models (ECM), where battery terminal voltage prediction is based on resolution of an electrical circuit, as in [8,9], thus obtaining a voltage prediction with an accuracy higher than 20mV. Largely spread are also lumped and empirical models [10,11], where heat generation prediction is usually based on simplified correlations [12,13]. Such models result particularly used in 3D simulations due to the lower number of battery parameters needed than traditional Newman pseudo-two dimensions (P2D) model [14]. Some of these approaches require a large number of parameters to describe accurately the module electrical and thermal behavior, which imply expensive tests for characterizing a single cell [15–18], that could also result in intensive destructive tests [19]. Since running such simulations is costly and time consuming, a model order reduction (MOR) technique is usually implemented to derive a lighter and more agile computational tool able to predict andcontrol the cell temperature in real time [20] and for deriving it many approaches can be found in the literature, e.g. equivalent thermal array based models [21], physical based [22] or empirically derived [23].

This paper presents a thermal investigation on a module of 20 prismatic lithium-titanate (LTO) cells air-cooled in two conditions: forced ventilation, later on dubbed fans on, and natural ventilation, dubbed fans off. First, a numerical 3D thermal model was built to investigate the temperature distribution inside the module in both fans on and fans off. Considering that the 3D model had to yield a concise description of the heat transfer inside the module, a simple thermal resistance model was used to describe the heat transfer inside the cells, whereas a computed fluid-dynamic (CFD) model implemented in COMSOL Multiphysics® analyzed the temperature and flow fields inside the module. The complete thermal model provided the cell temperatures with either fans on and fans off. Regarding this last, being time dependent natural convection simulation in enclosures computational expensive, an easy empirical correlations based approach is presented. Then, the numerical results were validated against experimental data gained in a extensive test campaign over a dedicated test bench. Finally, a MOR technique was applied to obtain an agile 0-dimensional Simulink model able to identify the hottest cells and predict their evolution in both cooling conditions, fans on and fans off, which was implemented and experimentally validated.

The proposed approach is an example of thermal model simplification steaming from an easy testing characterization. Although this model is based on certain operational assumptions and represents an average of several factors, the experimental results show that the level of accuracy can be high. Even if not as accurate as many other models in the literature [9,10], simple representations such as the one under consideration allow for effective implementation on digital signal processors (DSPs) and microcontrollers.

The work presented in this paper is part of a wider program developed in collaboration between research groups of the University of Padua and operative entities (manufacturing companies and student competition teams). In particular, the company FIAMM Energy Technology SpA (Montecchio Maggiore, Italy) [24] collaborated to the work here presented, which is an expanded and revised version of a congress presentation [25].

This research investigated prismatic lithium-titanate oxide (LTO) cells with a cathode of LiNixCoyMn1-xO2 (NMC) and an anode of Li4/3Ti5/3O4 (LTO) [26], having a 20Ah capacity. Fig. 1a and b shows respectively a picture and a scheme with sizes of the cell. In Table 1, the cell parameters are reported as provided by the manufacturer.

The module under investigation consisted of 20 such cells in series so that the its nominal voltage was 48V. In Fig. 1c, a picture of the stack is reported, while in Fig. 1d the position of the thermocouples array is shown. The cells were connected in series and at the module terminals by soldered aluminium tags. The cells were arranged in two rows spaced with a 7mm air gap, while the cells in each row, presenting the narrow faces to the front and back module faces, were spaced each other with a 5mm air gap. An aluminium casing enclosed the module on all lateral faces, with two holes housing two air fans in the front face and two round grilles in the back face. The bottom and the upper faces consisted of plastic lids and the latter hosted the BMS electronics, covered by a polymeric bonnet (Fig. 1c). Seven stainless-steel rods ensured module mechanical stiffness. The module dimensions were 260mm × 270mm × 115mm. Each cell was provided with a K-type thermocouple (±1K): data collected from the array of 20 thermocouples represented in Fig. 2c and d were monitored via remote-controlled acquisition system. A picture of the thermocouples array is presented in Fig. 1d. Temperatures measured by sensor sA and sB represented the highest stack temperature in fans off and fans on conditions, thus being the ones considered for validation.

The test campaign was carried out at the UNIPD Electrochemical Energy Storage and Conversion Laboratory (EESCoLab). The test bench allowed to run tests at 20°C room temperature, and consisted of a Dana Power Supplies (Torino, Italy) D2AX-M 85-75 battery charger/ electronic load (0–85V, -75/+75A, max power 4.5kW) and a fan controller (Fig. 3). Experiment control was provided by a control and monitoring system (MS) consisting of in-house software implemented NI-LabVIEW, NI data interface and a desktop pc, which controlled the module electric load condition and processed the thermocouple signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Module 3-D air flow and thermal model

ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve a better understanding of the thermal behavior in the whole volume inside the module, a CFD model was developed, capable of dealing with both fans on forced ventilation and fans off natural convection, the latter making sense in the case of low electric load and overheating. However, implementing a numerical model capable of facing both conditions posed non trivial challenges. The relevant physics calls for different approaches in the two cases:

ce:list-item.ce:para: In the fans on case, it was assumed that heat exchange was mainly driven by forced convection enforced by the fans. So, the air velocity field inside the module was first solved in stationary condition, providing the input data for the time-dependent solution of the temperatures field (such approach is presented in [27] and in [28]).

In the fans off case, a similar decoupling assumption was not acceptable. In fact, in natural convection the air flow strongly depends on the temperature evolution. In addition, when the fans are off the internal air volume is still in connection with the outer air through the grille openings at the module front and back faces. This problem was resolved resorting to Boussinesq’s approximation in analyzing a single cell in open air [29] and in studying a simple and small module [30]. Conversely, computing the fully time-dependent natural convection fields with these hypothesis for a much more complex geometry as the one of the module under investigation would have resulted in an unacceptable computing time on the PRECISION T5610 (12 cores, 2.10GHz, 48GB RAM) workstation used in the analysis. To overcome such challenge, an empirical correlations approach was used. In particular, the convectively enhanced conductivity correlations available in COMSOL Multiphysics® Heat Transfer module were used to model the heat exchange between the cells narrow faces in front of the grille opening and the outer air volume [31]. An example of such approach in a similar case is reported in [32].

ce:section.ce:para: In this paragraph, the model equations and the assumptions both for the fans on and the fans off operation are presented.

When the fans are working, the flow is convection driven, so that to determine the flow regime inside the module the Reynolds number was considered:
(1)
R e =
ρ
a i r
u
i n
d
i n
μ
a i r
≈ 4840
where
ρ
a i r
and
μ
a i r
are the air density and dynamic viscosity, respectively (Table 2),
d
i n
= 0.0768m is the fan inlet diameter, and
u
i n
= 1ms-1 is the air inlet velocity (determined through an anemometer measurement). This value of the Reynolds number indicated that the flow is turbulent in proximity of the fan. Therefore, to capture the flow behavior in the entire module volume, the turbulent k- ɛ realizable model was chosen due to its robustness [28]. The air was assumed incompressible because the velocities of the cooling flow are anyhow low [33], so that the mass continuity (2) and momentum equations (3) could be written respectively as [34]:
(2)

model constants [32,35].

The thermal field was modeled by the power balance equation [34,36]:
(8)
ρ

∂ T

where ρ ,
c
p
, μ , and λ are the medium density, heat capacity, viscosity, and thermal conductivity, respectively (Table 2). Here,
q
s
is the volume specific heat generation term and
Θ
c e l l
is the single battery volume:
(9)

1

R

− I
( t )
T
∂
V
0
∂ T
It is worth noting as
q
s
is non-zero only inside the cells and accounts for the irreversible heat generation due to ohmic losses and the reversible heat generation due to entropy change.

R
i n t
is the internal resistance of the cell that depends on the state of charge (SOC), on the cell temperature, and on the cell load condition either in charge or discharge,
I
( t )
is the module current and
∂
V
0
/
∂ T
is the cell SOC-dependent temperature derivative of the open circuit voltage, that accounts for the entropy change. In order to determine the cell average internal heat generation, we performed a galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) test at ambient temperature, characterized by 10 current pulses (40A for 3 min each), first in discharge and then in charge, starting from
S O C =
100%, as reported in Fig. 4. According to the test, we assumed a constant cell internal resistance
R
i n t
equal to 1.5mΩ. Such simplification, that is in agreement to the manufacturer data-sheet for the considered temperature range, is largely spread in literature [39,40] and fits the need for fast modeling of the battery pack without complex cell characterization tests [9]. The behavior of the OCV temperature derivative versus SOC for LTO cell are given in [41].

Due to the module geometrical symmetry with respect to the mid vertical plane, such was assumed the temperature distribution and only half module was modeled. The geometrical model of the half module was built with Autodesk® Inventor 2022, while the physical model and the mesh were generated with COMSOL 6.0 Multiphysics®. During the fans on condition, the fans action was assumed as the driving force of air convection in the half module and, following the decoupled approach, the stationary flow field was solved first and then the time-dependant temperature field was computed based on the flow field results [28]. This assumption allowed another simplification: considering that the air volume is symmetric with respect to the mid horizontal plane, the flow field was solved only in one half of the modeled volume (one quarter of the module volume) and then mirrored on the other half, which resulted in a substantial reduction of the computational burden.

When the fans are not working, the module was cooled by exchanging heat with outside steady air. The air inside the module was assumed to be steady and thermal field was modeled by (8) and (9). The boundary conditions of the thermal model in fans off conditions are quite different from those in fans on. A strict modeling approach would require a transient solving of the air velocity field inside all the gaps of the battery pack caused by the natural convection phenomena. Such approach would need a specific meshing strategy and relevant computational resources and time, and it is rarely developed for complex geometries like the one considered in this work [29,42]. To take into account the heat exchange between the cell narrow sides facing the outer air volume through the grille openings, the following convectively enhanced conductivity correlations were considered [31,43]:
(10)
q = − k N u
∇ T
(11)
N u = 0 . 22
P r
0 . 2 + P r
R
a
L
0 . 28
H
L
− 0 . 25
where
N u
is the Nusselt number,
P r =
μ
a i r
c
p , a i r
/
k
a i r
is the Prandtl number, H is the height of the internal volume, L is the width of the gap between cells and grille and
R
a
L
is the pertinent Rayleigh number:
(12)
R
a
L
=
ρ
a i r
2
g
|
α
p
|
c
p , a i r
μ
a i r
λ
a i r
Δ T
L
3
with g the gravitational acceleration,
α
p
the air thermal expansion coefficient [38],
λ
a i r
the thermal conductivity,
Δ T
the temperature difference between the cell faces and the grille wall that is at
T
a m b
. The module under investigation presented L
≈ 0.01m, H
≈ 0.08m and, taking an average temperature difference
Δ
T
a v g
= 6°C, we get
P r ≈
0.77 and
R
a
L
≈
1000 were obtained. Since the Nusselt number can also be written as
N u = h L /
λ
a i r
, a heat transfer coefficient h
≈ 2.04Wm-2 K-1 was deduced.

The boundary conditions were imposed in accordance with the experimental conditions applied during the experiments at EESCoLab test bench, to ensure consistent model validation conditions. Different boundary conditions sets were adopted according with the two fans operating modes: some of them have been already specified in Sections 4.2.1 and 4.2.2. For the fans on operating state, an homogeneous velocity of 1ms-1 was imposed at the fan inlet, while the outlet grille was modeled imposing no backflow condition, homogeneous pressure and localized pressure drop characterized by
K
l o s s
equal to 0.3. It was applied a non-slip flow condition on all internal walls except the middle horizontal and vertical planes, where symmetry conditions were applied. As regards the thermal field, the bottom and upper surfaces were considered adiabatic, while a heat transfer coefficient of 3Wm-2 K-1 was imposed on the lateral faces to account for natural convection with the ambient air [44]. Symmetry thermal boundary conditions were applied on the middle vertical plane. The inlet airflow was imposed at constant ambient temperature of 25°C, while a thermal open boundary condition was applied at the outlet. As previously described, in the fans off condition no flow field is modeled, while inner module air volume heat exchange is computed through empirical correlations described in Section 4.2.2. As like as in the fans on case, also in fans off the module bottom and upper surfaces were considered adiabatic, while a heat transfer coefficient of 3Wm-2 K-1 was imposed to the side module wall.

Due to the high module geometrical complexity, two computational meshes were built in COMSOL Multiphysics®: one dedicated to the fluid flow computation (used only for the fans on case) and one for the thermal computation (common for the fans on and the fans off condition). The first mesh was a physics-based mesh of 813238 elements, presenting 508645 tetrahedra, 321 pyramids and 304272 prisms, optimized for flow field resolution using a boundary layer refinement along the non-slip walls. The second mesh was a user-defined mesh of 776217 elements, having 676155 tetrahedra, 30308 pyramids, 80 prisms and 69674 hexahedra. These mesh sizes were the outcome of an optimized trade-off between solution accuracy and computational costs. In fact, the mesh independence study showed a computational error lower than 1% for the fans on case on the sB probe at t equal to 1620s varying the mesh elements number as reported in Fig. 5.

As stated above, a decoupled approach was considered in the case the fans on operation. The stationary flow field solution was obtained using a segregated approach with pseudo time-stepping settings where the relative tolerance was set at
1 × 1
0
− 3
. In order to obtain a stable and robust solution, a 9-steps parametric ramping of the inlet velocity was performed, up to the value of 1ms-1. The obtained stationary flow pattern was used as input for the calculation of the time-varying temperature field, together with the domain initial temperatures and the module load profile. For the computation of both the fans on and fans off time-varying temperatures fields, an intermediate time-stepping approach with a maximum time step of 10s was used.

The model was validated comparing the CFD results against the measurements obtained at EESCoLab test bench. Due to the complexity of the pack open geometry, fans on flow field validation through pressure drops measurements could have resulted unreliable, so as in [7] and in [45] a direct comparison on cell temperatures was done. In both fans on and fans off validation tests the current nominal value was kept constant at 40A, equal to 2C, so twice the constant current that would discharge the nominal battery capacity in one hour during both charge and discharge and the temperatures from the sA and sB thermocouples (Fig. 2c and d) were considered, since the former presented the hottest measured temperature in the fans off mode, while the latter had the hottest measured temperature in the fans on mode. The load profile considered have different shape (2C constant current (CC)-constant voltage (CV) at 2.7V discharge, 2C CC charge for the fans on, 2C CC discharge, 2C CC–1.5V CV discharge for the fans off) to emulate different common-used working condition. Such load profiles are similar to the ones used in [7,34]. The temperatures profiles presented in Fig. 6, show that the computed values were in good agreement with experimental data: the average absolute error
Δ ɛ
was lower than 0.3°C in the fans on mode and equal to 0.52°C in the fans off mode.
Fig. 7 shows the air velocity magnitude and temperature distributions on the middle horizontal half plane during the 40A test in fans on mode.

The air velocity magnitude (Fig. 7a) increases along the gaps between the cells rows and is markedly different among gaps, being higher in the three channels in front of the fan. The temperatures distribution (Fig. 7b) shows that the first row cells reach lower temperatures, except the two not aligned with the fan. The highest temperature is found in the cell provided with the thermocouple sB in the second row.

Fig. 8 shows the velocity streamlines and the internal surfaces temperature color map in the same operating condition, highlighting that the air flow distributes unevenly inside the module, with the cells closer to the external walls cooled by a weaker air flow, which results in higher temperatures compared to the other cells.

MOR is an approach to simplify the burden of heavy numerical models in order to obtain more agile simulation tools. In particular, lumped models allows to simplify the simulation of spatially distributed physical systems because, under certain assumptions, such systems can be approximated by relatively simple topologies consisting of discrete elements. This approach is widely used in electronics [46], hydraulics, mechanics, and other engineering areas.

In our case, this approach allows the transition from the power balance expressed by Eq. (8) to a finite parameter equation in which the space dependence vanishes so that, instead of integrating such partial differential equation (PDE), a simpler and more effective procedure can be adopted, in which the power density balance (W m–3) considered in (8) is replaced with a power balance (W) extended over entire volume of the cell and consisting of four terms: one proportional to the derivative of temperature describing the cell thermal capacity, one proportional to the cell temperature difference with external environment describing the heat exchanges, one describing the heat generated by the cell Joule losses, and one describing the cell reversible entropic heat change.

The following ordinary differential equation (ODE) is obtained:
(13)
C
1
∂ T
( t )
∂ t
+
C
2
(
ω
F
)
( T
( t )
−
T
a m b
)
= =
R
i n t
I
( t )
2
−
C
3
I
( t )
T
∂
V
0
( S o C )
∂ T
where
C
1
,
C
2
and
C
3
are constants to be determined.
C
1
takes into account the thermal inertia of the cell while
C
2
(
ω
f
)
is an overall contribution coming from the heat transfer coefficient. In the case under analysis, this is a function of the fan angular speed
ω
f
(and other structural factors e.g. module geometry and medium properties).
R
i n t
I
2
is the cell average ohmic generation source, being
I
( t )
the cell current, positive when charging the battery. The entropic term
∂
V
0
/ ∂ T
constitutes an element in the balance of Eq. (13), that is sized by the coefficient
C
3
. Identifying the coefficients of Eq. (13) is crucial in obtaining an accurate MOR cell characterization. Some of these coefficient are easily accessible from the data provided by the manufacturer, but this is not case of others and determining them can be tricky, also because they may be highly dependent on the operating conditions. Therefore, an experimental identification of these coefficients had to be carried out. It is important to note that the following characterization is generic. In practice, the proposed lumped model can be employed to forecast the temperature evolution at the critical point. Consequently, experimental data required for the characterization of Eq. (13) must be gathered specifically for that critical point. The following lumped model has been implemented on MATLAB Simulink software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental calibration of the MOR parameters

ce:section.ce:para:
This coefficient acts as the average heat capacitance of the cell and its value was calculated from the specific heat capacity
c
p
and the cell mass m , both parameters having been provided by the manufacturer, in the case under investigation. Assuming
c
p
constant over the cell volume, it was calculated as
C
1
= m
c
p
.

To identify the remaining coefficients of Eq. (13), four experimental tests were conceived and carried out: Ide-Test1, Ide-Test2, Ide-Test3, and Ide-Test4. These tests combined discharging, charging and resting phases in two different fan modes: fan-on at maximum speed
(
ω
f
=
ω
f , m a x
) and fan-off (
ω
f
= 0
). The fan operating conditions in the characterization tests are summarized in Table 3. The combinations of the state of the fans was chosen so that they are always on or always off, as in Ide-Test1 and Ide-Test2, or so that they change state when entering the resting phase, as in Ide-Test3 and Ide-Test4. This provides two tests for each operating condition (i.e., fan on and fan off during the resting phase) each starting from a different initial temperature value and overall conditions. Other combinations are clearly possible, but for the purposes of identification these four cases are more than sufficient.

In the resting phase, the current is zero,
I
r e s
= 0
A
, and Eq. (13) reduces to:
(14)
C
1
∂ T
( t )
∂ t
+
C
2
T
( t )
−
T
a m b
= 0
The solution of this linear first-order ODE is the cell thermal relaxation in free evolution:
(15)
T
( t )
=
T
a m b
+
(
T
0
−
T
a m b
)
e
−
t
τ
r
where
τ
r
=
C
1
/
C
2
(
ω
f
)
is the first order time constant, which depend on the test modes.

Fig. 9 shows the resting time-profile obtained from the experimental tests Ide-Test1-4, from which the values of
T
0
and
τ
r
where numerically deduced. Being the value of
C
1
was known,
C
2
(
ω
f
)
was obtained as
C
2
(
ω
f
)
=
C
1
/
τ
r
. Specifically, Ide-Test2 and Ide-Test3 were used to extract
C
2
(
ω
f
=
ω
f , m a x
)
while Ide-Test1 and Ide-Test4 are considered to determine
C
2
(
ω
f
= 0 )
.

This coefficient is related to the simplifications involved in the lumped model. Eq. (13) derives from the density energy balance, while the lumped form consider volume integral quantities, and
C
3
takes into account such extension to the cell volume.
C
3
was computed imposing the known values of
∂
V
0
/ ∂ T
(Section 4.2) in Eq. (13). Its value was fitted using a least squares method that minimized the errors between the numerical results and experimental data of more testing points.

After the identification process, Eq. (13) was fully defined. To validate the developed lumped model and understand potential issues, a series of comparisons with experimental tests are carried out, consisting of four experimental tests: Val Test1, Val Test2, Val Test3, and Val Test4.

In Val–Test1 the fan was on (
ω
f
=
ω
f , m a x
) during the resting phase and off (
ω
f
= 0
) before. For this, the test current is 1C while for the following ones is 2C. This value is not determinant for the identification of the coefficients. The choice depended only on the measurement setup in use. Fig. 10a shows the cell current, the temperature measured and the corresponding numerical results. The maximum absolute error between measurements and simulations was
Δ
ɛ
m a x
< 1
∘ C
.

In Val–Test2 the fan was always off (
ω
f
= 0). Fig. 10b shows again the current and the temperature measured and simulated. An error that increases in time appears in this case. The final error between measurements and simulations is
Δ
ɛ
m a x
≈ 3
∘ C
.

In Val–Test3 the fan was maintained on throughout the test. Fig. 10c shows that the experimental behavior was well predicted by the simulation with a very small error (
Δ
ɛ
m a x
< 0 . 5
∘ C
).

In Val–Test4 cell was repeatedly discharged and charged, the fan remained active throughout the test except during the resting phase. Once again, Fig. 10d shows that the model results were in good accordance with the experimental curves (
Δ
ɛ
m a x
< 1
∘ C
).

The accuracy obtained with the numerical 3D and MOR models was generally satisfactory, proving that the proposed methods can effectively predict the thermal behavior of a LTO cell module like that here investigated. In all analyses, a constant room temperature
T
a m b
was assumed. This is a limitation when the case under investigation implies a separation between the module internal air volume and the external environment, even temporarily. This may happen when the cell is employed inside a structure with rather small access to the external environment. In such cases, the definition of room temperature should be modified.

In our tests, only Val–Test2 (Fig. 10b), shows a slightly larger discrepancy between measurements and predicted values. Such discrepancy depends on the operating conditions. Precisely, the value of
T
a m b
is assumed constant throughout the test. However, in the considered test case, this condition is not met. This justifies the different trend in the steady state situation described by the model compared with the experimental results. In fact, when the fans are off there was a separation between the volume of air at
T
a m b
and the volume of air exchanging with the cell. By replacing this constant value with the actual temperature of the air volume trapped inside the structure, the discrepancy disappears, as shown in Fig. 11a and b.

As a perspective strategy, rather than complicating the model by introducing a second time constant dynamics, a simpler approach could consist in substituting
T
a m b
with the temperature
T
i n t
( t )
measured inside the structure containing the cell. This approach is valid due to the different dynamics of the phenomena involved. In fact, the value of
T
a m b
varies much more slowly than the cell temperature. Therefore, it is possible to avoid the introduction of a second time constant by considering
T
a m b
to be time-varying with a law that is scrolled by the evolution of the other temperatures.

In the comparison shown in Fig. 11a,
T
a m b
is replaced with such
T
i n t
( t )
. This highlights that the identified time constant
τ
r
(which is independent of
T
a m b
) is correct and the model can provide an very good dynamic description also the fan is off. Fig. 11b shows
T
i n t
( t )
with a double sampled values: at the beginning of the test and just before the resting phase. The results show that even such coarse sampling of the internal temperature can significantly improve the description of the cell temperature dynamics.

The temperature distributions predicted with the developed 3-D CFD model demonstrated to fit experimental data at a level of accuracy comparable with other best-practice models presented in the literature [7]. The computed air flow fields inside the module have a pivotal role in understanding the reasons of the temperatures spread among the cells in the fans on cooling condition. In fact, velocity magnitude values vary widely between the higher flow rate in the central air gaps, where the temperatures lower, and the recirculation occurring at corners, where heat is not effectively transferred to the outer air. The presence of outlet grilles enhances this temperatures unevenness, opposing to the outflow of hotter air. The fans off condition was modeled through empirical correlations that took into account the convectively enhanced conduction effects between the outer cells surfaces and the external air. Such cooling condition should be avoided for long cycling operations. The 3D fans on and fans off models showed to predict the stack cells temperature with an average error lower than 1°C, thus showing the effectiveness of the quick and straightforward method adopted.

The 0-D simplified lumped thermal model of the module implemented in Simulink and derived from energy balance equations proved effective in describing the thermal behavior of the hottest cells in both fans on and fans off conditions. This approach catches a convenient balance between complexity and accuracy, making it useful for thermal control applications in energy storage systems. Analyzing charge, discharge, and resting phases, while accounting for variations in fan speed contributes to a detailed understanding of thermal dynamics.

The experimental characterization process facilitated the identification of crucial model parameters, encompassing thermal coefficients and factors quantifying heat transfer and internal dissipation, resulting in a flexible and simply-to-characterize model. The experimental validation showed a commendable alignment between numerical model results and the experimental data, with an average error of about 0.5°C. These outcomes underscore the model’s capacity to reliably predict the thermal behavior of batteries.

Nicolò Zatta: Investigation, Methodolody, Data curation, Software, Validation, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Giovanni Bonanno: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Andrea Trovò: Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Giovanni Cristofoli: Investigation, Supervision. Paolo Mattavelli: Methodology, Supervision, Conceptualization. Massimo Guarnieri: Methodology, Conceptualization, Resources, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
